alternatives.content: Hi, I'm Amy from Noggin Builders, and I'm so excited to partner with our friends from Learning Resources to bring you some ideas for fun STEM exploring you can do at home.
Is your preschooler ready to learn to code?
Sure.
Of course, the best way for a preschooler to learn anything is through play.
There are a lot of great games and activities you can do with your preschooler to help build the skills that will help them become a coder.
Let's play a few.
One of the most important things to understand about coding is that computers can only do what they're told to do.
I like to play a game called Robot Says, similar to Simon Says, to help teach this idea.
So, let's start by making sure that your preschooler understands what a robot is.
You could say something like, a robot is a machine that helps humans do work.
A robot can only do the things that it's told to do.
The person that gives instructions to a robot is called a programmer.
Let's play a game where I'm the programmer and you're the robot.
Then, you're gonna give your preschooler commands, like, touch your head, touch your shoulders, bend over, touch your toes.
Just give one direction at a time.
That's what we're trying to teach with this game.
And unlike Simon says, there's no tricks.
We want our robot to do all of the actions that we ask it to.
And don't forget, give your preschooler a chance to be the programmer, and you be the robot.
They're sure to make you do some really silly things.
Sorting is another great activity to build your preschooler's critical thinking skills.
This is one of my favorite sorting toys because you can sort by shape or by color, and it has a building component because you stack the pieces.
We're gonna play a game called What's missing.
Let's start by warming up.
Pick two pieces and ask if your child can match the color.
Then try with another color or another shape.
Can your preschooler find all the stars?
You can add movement to this game by hiding the pieces around your area and send your child on a hunt to sort for all the reds or all the circles.
Now that we're warmed up, we're gonna turn this into a puzzle.
This puzzle's really gonna build your child's critical thinking skills.
So let's start by showing you how to build this puzzle.
Let's say the answer to the puzzle is blue triangle.
You place 2 or 3 blue items in a row, but not the blue triangle, and then 2 or 3 triangles in this column, but not the blue triangle.
See this empty spot where the row and column intersect.
It has to match both the row and column, so it must be a blue triangle.
The first time you play this game with your preschooler, you might have to explain how to solve the puzzle.
For example, you could say, see how these blocks are both blue, and these are both triangles.
In this empty spot, we need something that's both blue and a triangle.
You can make the puzzle as easy or as challenging as is right for your preschooler.
Soon enough, your preschooler will be making puzzles for you.
I hope you had a great time with these two games.
Next time, we'll talk about patterns and using maze games to learn about algorithms.
See you soon and happy exploring.
